title: How AI Is Rewriting Human Nature | NOEMA
gist: Shanghai-based essayist Jacob Dreyer argues that just as AI-driven biotech opens plausible paths to radically extended human lifespans, the world's most advanced AI hubs (Silicon Valley, Shanghai, Seoul, Singapore, New York) are experiencing collapsing birthrates, and traces how divergent US and Chinese transhumanist visions — escaping the body vs. redesigning it — are reshaping reproduction, health and selfhood.

insights.claim: AI's growing ability to map human proteins and optimize sleep, diet and exercise is making significant increases in human longevity a plausible near-term goal, driving a widespread culture of biohacking (supplements, extreme diets, wearable step-tracking) among urban professionals.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Frames AI-enabled longevity science (protein mapping, personalized health optimization) as a maturing, investable category rather than fringe wellness.
insights.locator: But the promise of AI for science

insights.claim: Scientists are accelerating brain-computer interface (BCI) research that uses AI to convert brain signals into artificial outputs, with some pursuing the eventual goal of uploading human consciousness into a durable digital existence.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: BCI-for-consciousness-upload is moving from sci-fi to a stated research agenda among credentialed scientists — a frontier to track for both medical and speculative-tech capital.
insights.locator: Scientists are ramping up their explorations

insights.claim: The author frames biology as potentially the first domain where AI genuinely understands the world rather than merely predicting it, suggesting AI's first cosmic-scale impact will be on human bodies themselves.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A distinct thesis for where AI's real-world impact concentrates first — biology/health, not just knowledge work — useful for sector-allocation debates.
insights.quote: Biology may be the first domain where AI genuinely understands the world rather than merely predicting it.
insights.locator: I've come to believe that the first place

themes.theme: The fertility collapse paradox in AI hubs

insights.claim: A National Bureau of Economic Research study found that early smartphone access in the US contributed to lower birthrates, linking the rise of consumer computation directly to fertility decline.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A citable empirical link between consumer-tech penetration and demographic decline, relevant to any long-horizon labor-supply or pension-liability model.
insights.locator: A recent study from the National Bureau

insights.claim: Silicon Valley's birthrate is 9.7 births per 1,000 people, significantly below the rest of the US and dropping sharply in recent years.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the demographic cost concentrated in the highest-income tech labor market, a data point for regional labor-supply and housing-demand forecasts.
insights.locator: In Silicon Valley, the birthrate of 9.7

insights.claim: South Korea has the world's lowest birthrate at around five births per 1,000 people, while China's birthrate has fallen to a record low of 5.63 per 1,000.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Anchors the severity of East Asian demographic decline with specific figures — directly relevant to long-term consumption, pension and labor-force models for both economies.
insights.locator: In other high-tech places like South Korea

insights.claim: The cities with the lowest birthrates globally — Shanghai, San Francisco, New York, Seoul and Singapore — are also the world's leading AI and technology hubs, sharing high housing costs and work-centered cultures of meaning and status.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Suggests AI/tech-hub economic success and demographic collapse are correlated, not coincidental — a headwind for these cities' long-run domestic consumer and housing markets.
insights.locator: Shanghai, San Francisco, New York, Seoul, Singapore

insights.claim: In 2025 the global fertility rate reached what the UN called an 'unprecedented decline,' and the author speculates that the arrival of AI as a historical rupture could accelerate the collapse of reproduction's perceived value.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A speculative but headline-relevant forward claim: AI adoption itself, not just smartphones/social media, may further depress fertility — worth tracking against future UN fertility data releases.
insights.locator: In 2025, the global fertility rate

insights.claim: Democratic systems that rely on elevating the 'median man,' like the American order, face acute political strain from collapsing birthrates, while hierarchical systems like China's appear more externally stable even though their base population is similarly unwilling to reproduce.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A political-economy framing worth watching: demographic collapse may erode democratic legitimacy faster than authoritarian legitimacy, even where underlying fertility trends are similar.
insights.locator: Systems based on elevating the median man

insights.claim: American transhumanism seeks to escape the body, treating the brain as a house for the soul (e.g., BCI-driven consciousness upload), while Chinese transhumanism assumes the body is permanent and must instead be redesigned or fixed.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A clear investable-thesis split: US biotech/BCI capital chases mind-uploading and escape-the-body tech, while Chinese biotech capital chases body-engineering and regenerative medicine — different company archetypes to back in each market.
insights.quote: If American transhumanism seeks to escape the body and considers the brain a sort of house for the soul, Chinese transhumanism assumes the body will never leave us and therefore must be redesigned or fixed.
insights.locator: If American transhumanism seeks to escape

insights.claim: China's state-led biotech push treats the aging population (over a quarter expected to be above age 60 by 2040) as an engineering input to be maximized, reflecting a socialist-industrial legacy comfortable with large-scale human experimentation by both state and citizens.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: China's aging crunch (25%+ over 60 by 2040) is explicitly driving state biotech policy — a structural tailwind for Chinese biotech/longevity firms operating with fewer regulatory constraints than US peers.
insights.locator: As the population rapidly ages

insights.claim: The author attributes greater Chinese public and elite optimism about AI and biotech, relative to American skepticism, to trust that the Chinese state will rein in malfunctioning tech (e.g., pulling self-driving cars off roads) versus a perception that the US government is aligned with Big Tech rather than public safety.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Regulatory-trust asymmetry may let Chinese consumers adopt AI/biotech products faster than skeptical US consumers, shaping which market scales new health tech first.
insights.locator: In a general sense, it is striking

insights.claim: At Shanghai's Huashan Hospital, neuroscientist Ying Mao's BCI research lets stroke, spinal-cord-injury and Parkinson's patients control devices or better respond to questions via wires attached directly to the brain.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term clinical BCI use case (motor/cognitive rehabilitation) already deployed in China, ahead of the more speculative consciousness-upload narrative.
insights.locator: Back in Huashan Hospital in January

insights.claim: Chinese 'embodied AI' models built into humanoid robots and automobiles are being trained directly on human-body data and are advancing shockingly quickly, according to the author.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Flags China's embodied-AI/humanoid-robotics pace as a specific area of rapid capability growth worth tracking against Western competitors.
insights.locator: At the same time, Chinese

insights.claim: Bryan Johnson opened a longevity clinic in Shanghai and held his 'Don't Die' summit there exploring longevity, health and the future of humanity, exemplifying a wave of body-obsessed Californians traveling to China in search of stem-cell and other treatments.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: US longevity entrepreneurs are actively relocating activity to China to access treatments unavailable domestically — signals regulatory arbitrage in the longevity industry.
insights.locator: Bryan Johnson, the boy-king of immortality

insights.claim: Bay Area tech workers are consuming experimental Chinese-manufactured research peptides sourced informally (e.g., via Instagram), even though the same Chinese sellers refuse to sell them domestically in Shanghai because the drugs are not approved by the Chinese government.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Highlights a live regulatory gap: unapproved Chinese research chemicals are flowing into unregulated US biohacker use — a compliance and public-health risk building beneath the longevity-supplement trend.
insights.locator: On Instagram, I got in touch with Chinese peptide

insights.claim: San Francisco's Frontier Tower and AGI House, hubs for AI and biotech startups, host events like a 'Chinese peptide rave' where unapproved research chemicals similar to Ozempic precursors are used to purportedly enhance cognition, sleep and memory.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Shows biohacking culture is institutionalizing inside AI-industry physical infrastructure (co-working/lab spaces), not staying fringe — a cultural signal for the sector's risk appetite.
insights.locator: In mid-January, I visited San Francisco's Frontier Tower

insights.claim: While overall birthrates collapse, the wealthiest and most knowledgeable individuals — the author cites Elon Musk having more than a dozen children — are having far more children than is typical, making personal health and reproduction an emerging elite technology in the rich world.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Points to a widening demographic bifurcation where fertility itself becomes a marker (and tool) of elite status and resource access — relevant to wealth-concentration and dynastic-capital narratives.
insights.locator: Not everyone has decided to stop having children

insights.claim: In China, feminists describe a 'birth strike' as an implicit protest against centralized state power, while South Korea's 4B movement rejects marriage, dating, childbirth and sex altogether, both framed by the author as reactions against patriarchal, high-competition societies that pressure women to reproduce without making life more livable.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Frames fertility collapse partly as organized social resistance, not just economic rationality — implying pro-natalist policy alone (subsidies, exhortation) is unlikely to reverse the trend without addressing gender and power dynamics.
insights.locator: China's dismal birthrate hangs over it all

insights.claim: Some in China express casual willingness for the country's population to shrink from 1.4 billion to a few hundred million, provided the remaining people are the 'right' people — reflecting transhumanism in China as a post-socialist industrial theology aimed at a perfected collective body.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A striking anecdotal data point on elite Chinese tolerance for severe depopulation if paired with genetic/quality selection — relevant context for China's long-run demographic and eugenics-adjacent policy debates.
insights.locator: In China, I've heard casual comments

insights.claim: The author frames declining urban fertility as a rational shift away from treating children as embodied capital (as in agrarian societies), since city-dwellers increasingly judge the 'return on investment' of raising children to be poor amid an uncertain future.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: An economic framework for fertility decline (children as low-ROI capital) that implies pro-natalist policy must change the actual returns to parenting, not just subsidize marginally.
insights.locator: Rather than farmers having a flock of kids

insights.claim: The author argues the American 'individual' — the Enlightenment concept underpinning the postwar middle-class configuration of house, yard, car and self-improvement, sustained by cheap energy, defense dominance and manageable technology — is dissolving as AI, biotech and planetary-scale systems advance.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: A macro-cultural thesis that the AI/biotech era is eroding the individualist consumer identity that underpinned 20th-century American economic and political structure — relevant to long-horizon consumer and political-risk narratives.
insights.locator: America built its civilization around the idea

insights.claim: The author closes on the unresolved question of governance: humans are certain to change under AI and biotech, but who gets to decide what people become remains an open and contested question.
insights.speaker: JACOB DREYER
insights.so_what: Frames the coming regulatory and ethical battle over human-enhancement technology as a governance question, not merely a technical one — worth tracking for future policy/regulatory catalysts in biotech and AI.
insights.quote: Humans are going to change — of that there is no doubt. What remains unsettled is who gets to decide what people become.
insights.locator: Humans are going to change
